ML CPU Scheduler
How It Works Try It Algorithms Architecture

CPU scheduling that learns from data

Traditional schedulers use fixed rules. This one uses a RandomForest model to predict burst times and schedule smarter. Run the simulator below to see actual numbers.

Run the Simulator View Source

How it works

Three steps. No magic.

1

Generate processes

Random processes are created with arrival times, burst times, and priorities — just like a real OS workload.

2

Predict burst times

A trained RandomForest model predicts how long each process will take, using arrival time as input.

3

Schedule and compare

All six algorithms run on the same process set. You see the actual waiting times and turnaround times side by side.

Interactive simulator

Configure, run, and compare all six algorithms on the same data.

Generated processes

PID Arrival Burst Priority

Results

Comparison

Average Waiting Time

Average Turnaround Time

Gantt charts

The six algorithms

Each one handles process scheduling differently. Here's what each actually does.

FCFS

Non-preemptive

First Come First Serve. Processes run in arrival order. Simple, but long processes block short ones.

Time complexity O(n log n)

SJF

Non-preemptive

Shortest Job First. Picks the process with the smallest burst time. Gives the best average wait — if you know burst times in advance.

Time complexity O(n²)

Round Robin

Preemptive

Each process gets a fixed time slice. Fair, but lots of context switches. Performance depends on the quantum size.

Time complexity O(n × total_burst / quantum)

Priority

Non-preemptive

Processes with higher priority (lower number) run first. Can starve low-priority processes indefinitely.

Time complexity O(n²)

SRTF

Preemptive

Shortest Remaining Time First. Like SJF, but preempts the running process if a shorter one arrives. Very responsive.

Time complexity O(n × max_burst)

ML-Based

Machine Learning

Uses a RandomForest model to predict burst times from arrival time, then sorts by predicted burst — like SJF, but without needing to know burst times upfront.

Model RandomForestRegressor

Project structure

How the codebase is organized.

main.py Entry point — runs all schedulers and plots results
↓
schedulers/ fcfs, sjf, round_robin, priority, srtf, ml_scheduler
models/ train_model.py, model.pkl (RandomForest)
↓
utils/ process_generator — creates random workloads
results/ graphs.py, gantt.py — matplotlib visualizations

Built with

Python Core scheduling logic
scikit-learn RandomForest model
Pandas Data handling
NumPy Numerical operations
Matplotlib Charts and Gantt diagrams

ML-Based CPU Scheduling — A machine learning approach to OS process scheduling.
